rules.message: Network call made inside a loop (for/for-of/for-in/while/do, or an Array forEach/map/filter/reduce callback) — fires one request per iteration, the HTTP analogue of N+1 (rate-limit / serial-latency risk). Batch via a bulk endpoint, or gather inputs and issue one request (or a bounded Promise.all). Comment lines are skipped via `skip_comment_lines`, so a JSDoc comment merely documenting a network-call regex/example does not fire. The loop-token pattern is anchored to actual loop/iteration syntax (`for (`, `for await`, `while (`, `do {`, `.forEach(`/`.map(`/`.filter(`/`.reduce(`), not a bare `\bfor\b`/`\bdo\b` word match — an earlier version matched loop keywords inside ordinary prose string literals (e.g. `"logged in to do this"` matched `\bdo\b`) and inside template-literal text like `` `waiting for ${x}` ``. A loop body that also contains `retry`/`retries`/`backoff`/`attempt` is vetoed outright (a bounded retry loop around one call is the recommended remedy already applied, not the N+1 this rule targets). Test/e2e/fixture paths are excluded outright (deployed-surface rule — a test harness deliberately hitting a mock endpoint in a loop is not a production rate-limit risk). Precision limit: method-scan only proves a loop-shaped construct and a network call both occur somewhere in the same function body, not that the call is nested inside the loop's own block — an unrelated `.map()` transform elsewhere in the same function, before or after the loop, still co-occurs and counts. Suppress with `// api-in-loop-ok:` on the call's own line or the line directly above — the marker must sit immediately above the call.
